steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student concludes that similarity drives cross-category purchasing of the same brand. The question asks which finding would most directly weaken that causal claim. To weaken a causal conclusion, we need an alternative explanation for the observed correlation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Products in high-similarity pairs tended to be among the most expensive...': Price difference between pairs does not explain why cross-category purchasing correlates with similarity. Irrelevant to the causal claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Consumers were more likely to purchase both products in high-similarity pairs...': This simply restates the original data the student used — it supports rather than weakens the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — 'High-similarity pairs tended to be in categories with relatively few competing products from other brands...': This introduces a confounding variable — limited competition, not similarity itself, could drive cross-category buying. If fewer rival brands are available, consumers naturally buy both items from the same brand. This alternative explanation undermines the student's causal claim. ✓
  • Choice D — 'The positive correlation was equally strong for low and high brand recognition pairs': This shows the similarity effect persists regardless of brand recognition, which strengthens (not weakens) the idea that similarity is the key driver. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
